name: Inclusive Assessment Design
description: UDL checkpoints most relevant when designing assessments that are inclusive and equitable. Based on CAST UDL Guidelines 3.0.

checkpoints.contextNote: For oral and interactive assessments — consider how students can vary their response mode, timing, and physical environment.
checkpoints.harmfulPractice: Enforce one-size-fits-all classroom positioning, student placement, sit/stand, response parameters. Prevent additional presentation time for those with Accessibility Plans.
checkpoints.helpfulPractice: Allow students to adjust their physical environment (e.g. flexible seating, classroom positioning, and lighting), and embed flexibility in their means of presentation and response (including digitally) (e.g. rate, timing, speed).

checkpoints.contextNote: Consider linguistic diversity and cultural bias in assessment language and instructions.
checkpoints.harmfulPractice: No consideration of ableist or discriminatory language. Not identifying how culture may influence presentation content and delivery.
checkpoints.helpfulPractice: Acknowledge the different cultures in the room, recognise students presenting in a second+ language, enable closed captioning.

checkpoints.contextNote: For collaborative assessments — explicitly scaffold group learning, shared goals, and interdependence.
checkpoints.harmfulPractice: Failure to address how students can ask for help; ignoring practices of socially shared regulation of learning.
checkpoints.helpfulPractice: Establish group agreements for collaboration; identify team roles, expectations, and collaborative learning goals; intentionally develop communities of learning.

checkpoints.contextNote: Support diverse ways of knowing and making meaning in analytical tasks.
checkpoints.harmfulPractice: Implying (i.e. not explicit) effective sequences to scaffold learning; Ignoring holistic and interconnected indigenous knowledge systems.
checkpoints.helpfulPractice: Progressive disclosure of chunked information to best explore different media; support a diversity of cognitive approaches to enhance comprehension.

checkpoints.contextNote: Enable diverse analytical and creative tools, including responsible AI use.
checkpoints.harmfulPractice: Preventing students from using emerging and innovative tools; failing to teach the foundational and underlying principles that transfer across tools.
checkpoints.helpfulPractice: Enable a diversity of analytical and reflective tools; develop competencies of students for responsible use of Gen-AI; evaluate students' critique of tools and synthesis across traditional and contemporary.

checkpoints.contextNote: Build in space for imagination, experimentation, and creative engagement.
checkpoints.harmfulPractice: Preventing time for imagination, experimentation, and wonder; allowing play to occur without self-regulation; limiting broad sensory and temporal engagement.
checkpoints.helpfulPractice: Foster a mindset of playfulness; require experimentation across physical and digital spaces; incorporate requirements for a diverse range of artistic experiences (e.g. music, film, visual, digital, AI-generated, etc.).
